title: Overfitting Risk in SFT
concept: If trained too long on the SFT set, the model will overfit to specific phrasing, losing its general world knowledge (catastrophic forgetting).

exercises.prompt: What does the abbreviation SFT stand for in the context of training AI assistants?
exercises.explain: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) helps teach the model how to act like a helpful assistant, but doing it too much leads to overfitting.

cards.title: Can an AI Study Too Hard?
cards.body: Can an AI study so hard for one specific test that it forgets how to speak normally? Yes! In AI training, there is a fine line between practice and memorization. Let's explore how too much training can actually make an AI perform worse.

cards.title: What is Overfitting in SFT?
cards.body: Overfitting in Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) happens when an AI is trained too much on a specific dataset. Instead of learning general concepts, it starts memorizing exact phrases and templates, which can lead to 'catastrophic forgetting'—losing the vast, general knowledge it learned earlier.

cards.title: The Living Room Puppy
cards.body: Imagine teaching a puppy to sit. If you only train it inside your quiet living room for weeks, it might learn to sit perfectly there, but completely freeze or ignore you at a busy park. Overfitting is like that—the model gets too used to its quiet 'training room' and forgets how to act in the real world.

cards.title: SFT Overfitting in Action
cards.body: If you train an AI on a dataset where every helpful answer starts with 'Sure, I can help with that!', an overfitted model might start adding that exact phrase to every single response, even when answering a sad or serious question where it doesn't make sense.

cards.title: Myth: More Training is Always Better
cards.body: People often think that the longer you train an AI model, the smarter it gets. In reality, training it for too long on the same SFT data makes it rigid, repetitive, and less capable of handling unique, real-world questions.

cards.title: Balanced vs. Overfitted AI
cards.body: Compare how a balanced model responds versus a model that has been overtrained (overfitted) on a specific SFT response style.

right.text: Repeats the same phrasing templates over and over, ignoring natural context.

left.text: Gives natural, diverse answers based on the context of the user's prompt.

exercises.prompt: What happens during 'catastrophic forgetting' in an overfitted AI model?

options.text: It loses its general world knowledge.
It becomes much better at creative writing.
It starts answering questions much faster.
It deletes its own operating system.

exercises.explain: Catastrophic forgetting occurs when the new, repetitive SFT training overrides the massive amount of general facts the model learned during its initial pre-training phase.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To get the best assistant model, you should train it on the SFT dataset for as long as possible.
exercises.explain: AI models need a balance. Too much SFT training makes them rigid and causes them to lose their general abilities.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about SFT training.
exercises.sentence: Overfitting happens when a model starts to memorizing templates instead of understanding general concepts.
exercises.explain: When trained too much on a specific SFT dataset, the model begins to memorize patterns instead of learning general rules.

exercises.prompt: A user inputs: 'My dog is sick today.' Which of these responses is a sign of an overfitted AI?

options.text: AI Response A: 'I am so sorry to hear that. I hope your dog feels better soon.'
AI Response B: 'Sure, I can help with that! Your dog is sick today.'
AI Response C: 'That sounds stressful. Make sure to consult a vet.'

exercises.explain: AI Response B shows overfitting because it blindly copies a template phrase ('Sure, I can help!') even when the user's prompt is a sad statement that doesn't ask for help.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary danger of training an AI model for too long on an SFT dataset?

options.text: The model becomes too fast at generating answers.
The model gains too much new common sense.
The model memorizes exact training phrases and forgets general knowledge.
The model completely stops responding to any input.

exercises.explain: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is a powerful tool, but overdoing it causes the model to memorize specific prompt patterns and forget its broad, pre-trained world knowledge.
